attributes.stem:
A 63-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-week history of generalised abdominal distension and weight loss. She has experienced 6 kg of unintentional weight loss over the last four months. She admits that she has neglected her diet and does not look after herself very well since her husband died one year ago. She has had pain and bloating after meals for the last few months. She has no other gastrointestinal symptoms. Her medical history includes a left mastectomy for carcinoma five years earlier. She has a 20 pack-year smoking history and drinks two glasses of wine a day.

On examination, she appears unkempt. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Her abdomen is distended, soft and dull to percussion. An abnormality of her umbilicus is noted (Image)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for the umbilical appearance?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation and examination findings in this case are highly suggestive of disseminated intra-abdominal malignancy with a metastatic deposit in the umbilicus, also known as a Sister Mary Joseph's nodule. This condition is virtually pathognomonic of advanced intra-abdominal or pelvic malignancy.

The key features supporting this diagnosis include:
1. Painless abdominal swelling with significant weight loss
2. Soft, distended abdomen dull to percussion, suggesting ascites
3. Presence of an umbilical mass
4. History of previous breast cancer, which could potentially metastasize

Sister Mary Joseph's nodules are most commonly associated with gastrointestinal malignancies (about 50% of cases), particularly pancreatic, gastric, and colorectal cancers. Gynecological cancers, especially ovarian and uterine, account for about 30% of cases. Other primary sources can include breast and lung cancers.

An umbilical concretion is an accumulation of sebum and keratin in the umbilicus, typically presenting as a dark, firm mass. While it can cause local irritation, it would not be associated with the systemic symptoms described in this case, such as weight loss and abdominal distension.

Omphalitis refers to inflammation of the umbilicus, usually seen in newborns. It typically presents with redness, swelling, and discharge from the umbilicus, often accompanied by fever. The patient's age and lack of inflammatory signs make this diagnosis unlikely.

A paraumbilical hernia occurs when abdominal contents protrude through a weakness in the abdominal wall near the umbilicus. While it can cause a visible bulge, it would typically be reducible and would not explain the patient's systemic symptoms or the appearance of the umbilical lesion in the image.

A urachal cyst is a congenital anomaly resulting from incomplete obliteration of the urachus, a canal that connects the bladder to the umbilicus in fetal life. These cysts typically present in childhood or young adulthood and are not associated with the clinical picture described here.

Given the patient's history of breast cancer, it's important to note that while breast cancer can metastasize to the umbilicus, it's less common than gastrointestinal or gynecological primaries. The presence of a Sister Mary Joseph's nodule warrants urgent investigation, including imaging studies and biopsy, to identify the primary malignancy and guide further management.
learningPoints: An umbilical metastatic deposit (Sister Mary Joseph's nodule) is almost always the result of disseminated intra-abdominal malignancy, most commonly from gastrointestinal or gynecological origins.
Sister Mary Joseph's nodule is an important clinical sign that should prompt thorough investigation for an underlying primary malignancy.
The presence of ascites, weight loss, and abdominal distension in conjunction with an umbilical nodule is highly suggestive of advanced intra-abdominal malignancy.
references: Gabriele R, Conte M, Egidi F, et al. Umbilical metastases: current viewpoint. World J Surg Oncol 2005;3:13. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC552325/

Sasai K, Kawamura M, Okumara K, Kawai Y. Radiation therapy for Sister Mary Joseph's Nodule: a review. Advances in Radiation Oncology. 2024;9(2). Available at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2452109423001495
